--------------------------------------------------------------
This founder spent 8 years escaping the Airtable clone trap 🇮🇳
--------------------------------------------------------------

Rachit Khator was working at a Fortune 500 company in the US in 2017, watching teams run serious workflows out of spreadsheets and wishing there were a better middle ground. So he quit in 2018, moved back to India, and started Stackby: a no-code platform that feels as simple as a spreadsheet, but is powerful enough for non-technical teams to build custom databases, workflows, and business apps. Eight years later, Stackby is at 7-figure ARR and growing 15-20% month over month in a category where Airtable owns most of the oxygen.

The biggest challenge was positioning. When you’re the second mover, users and search engines want to file you under “Airtable alternative” before you’ve explained your own philosophy. (In the full interview Rachit gets into how he overcame this.)

To me, Rachit's greatest skill is marketing. All the greatest marketers are experimenters, and Rachit is no exception. For example, instead of building a polished marketplace, Stackby manually shipped 20-30 basic templates, tracked organic traffic and duplication, then doubled down only on high-intent categories like CRM, marketing, and project tracking.

The full interview gets into that experiment, the focus-vs-flexibility trap, and why raising too early can weaken the discipline that makes indie products survive.

items.subject: [Action Advised] Ensure you trust all Google Trust Services Root CAs and ECDSA certificate chains before Jun 15, 2026
items.snippet: Review changes to intermediate CAs and TLS certificate types. MY CONSOLE Hello 클론, In Q2 2026, to improve efficiency, we will be updating the Transport Layer Security (TLS) certificate for many Google
items.body:
Hello 클론,

In Q2 2026, to improve efficiency, we will be updating the Transport Layer  
Security (TLS) certificate for many Google endpoints. The intermediate  
Certificate Authority (CA) and certificate type will shift from a RSA  
certificate chain and leaf certificate to an ECDSA certificate.

What you need to know

Key changes:

   Many services, including googleapis.com, will shift to using the Google  
Trust Services WE1 intermediate and ECDSA based TLS certificates.

Potential impact:

   If your client applications are not configured correctly, they may be  
unable to connect to Google services after this change. Connection failures  
are most likely to occur in the following two scenarios:
    - Certificate pinning: We do not recommend pinning intermediate or leaf  
certificates. This practice will cause your application to break during  
routine certificate rotations.
    - Custom trust stores: If you use a custom trust store, you must ensure  
it includes all of Google Trust Services (GTS) Root CAs to prevent outages.
For most customers, no action is required as a result of this change.  
However, if you run a custom trust store or pin CAs, you may need to take  
action. We’ve provided additional information to guide you through this  
change.

What you need to do

Action advised before June 15, 2026:

    - Ensure you trust all Google Trust Services Root CAs as detailed in  
this Google Trust Services Certificates documentation[1].
    - Pinning is discouraged. If you choose to pin, ensure that all Google  
Trust Services Roots and, if applicable, intermediates are in your pin list.

Timeline: Services will shift to new intermediates throughout late Q2 2026.

Impacted customers/accounts:

Any cloud project using a limited set of trusted roots or pinning  
certificates may experience issues.

We’re here to help

Please review the Google Trust Services[2] announcement for guidance on  
ensuring reliable connections to Google services.

If you have any questions or require assistance, please contact Google  
Cloud Support[3].

Thanks for choosing Google Cloud.

— The Google Cloud Team

[1] https://link-68.example
[2] https://link-69.example
[3] https://link-70.example

© 2026 Google LLC 1600 Amphitheatre Parkway, Mountain View, CA 94043

You’ve received this mandatory service announcement to update you about  
important changes to Google Cloud or your account.

----------------------------------------------------
He bet eight years on a native desktop Git client ⚙️
----------------------------------------------------

Tobias Günther and his cofounders started Tower out of a small web agency, building a Git GUI for themselves because the existing Git GUIs weren't good enough. Ten months of development, no desktop app experience, no product experience, and two months of runway left. They launched in 2011 anyway. Almost eight years later, Tower had crossed seven-figure ARR with 100,000+ customers, including developers inside Apple, Google, and Amazon. Saas․group acquired it in 2021.

The big calls were unfashionable. Tobias kept Tower native on macOS and Windows when cross-platform web tech would have been easier, betting developers would reward performance and polish. Then in 2018, he moved from one-time licenses to yearly subscriptions, a risky switch for a desktop app with years of existing users. That stubbornness became Tower's edge with the kind of developers who notice when tools feel native.

Tobias unpacks all of it, and then some, in our interview. Enjoy!

Read the full article → ( https://link-124.example )

Keep a public list of things you refuse to build.

From ThemeForest to $65k/mo 🧩
-----------------------------

Ajay Patel and his cofounder Vrushank didn’t start Clevision by trying to own the whole customer relationship. They started on Envato’s ThemeForest, where developers were already looking for admin templates. That marketplace gave them distribution, proof of demand, and cash flow before they had to build a platform of their own.

ThemeForest still generates $15-20k/month for them, and Clevision’s broader suite of developer tools now does $65k/mo.

Later, things got more difficult. ThemeForest had given them distribution, payments, and a steady stream of buyers. Moving to their own platform meant learning the stuff the marketplace had handled for them: acquisition, support, pricing, and direct customer relationships. But once they owned the customer, each new product could build on the same developer audience instead of starting from zero.
